questions.question: Which of the following best describes a "connected integration" in Notion Calendar?

options.text: A conferencing tool that is automatically available because it is linked to your calendar provider, like Google Meet.
options.explanation: That describes a default integration, not a connected one.

options.text: A tool you set up by logging in, which generates new conference links for each event you create in Notion Calendar.
options.explanation: Correct – connected integrations require you to log in (e.g., Zoom) and Notion creates a fresh link for each new event.

options.text: A custom URL you manually paste into the conferencing dropdown after adding it in Settings.
options.explanation: That describes a custom integration, not a connected one.

options.text: Any conferencing option that is not supported by Notion Calendar, such as FaceTime.
options.explanation: FaceTime is not supported at all, so it is not a connected integration.

questions.question: If an event contains a conferencing link both in its description and in the dedicated conferencing field, which link does Notion Calendar use when you click “Join meeting”?

options.text: The link from the dedicated conferencing field.
options.explanation: The description link takes precedence, not the field.

options.text: The link from the event’s description.
options.explanation: Correct – Notion Calendar prioritises the description link over the conferencing field.

options.text: Both links are opened in separate tabs.
options.explanation: Only one link is opened, and it’s the description link.

options.text: Notion Calendar shows an error and asks you to choose manually.
options.explanation: No error is shown; the description link is automatically used.

questions.question: What happens when you enable Personal Meeting IDs (PMIs) in your Zoom profile and then add a Zoom link via Notion Calendar?

options.text: Notion Calendar always generates a one‑time PIN regardless of the PMI setting.
options.explanation: When PMI is enabled, Notion uses the stable PMI instead of a one‑time PIN.

options.text: Notion Calendar uses the stable PMI and its passcode for the meeting link.
options.explanation: Correct – with PMI enabled, Notion Calendar pulls the stable PMI and associated passcode.

options.text: Notion Calendar disables Zoom integration because PMIs are not supported.
options.explanation: PMI support is built‑in; the integration remains functional.

options.text: Notion Calendar asks you to manually enter a passcode for each meeting.
options.explanation: Passcodes are automatically handled based on PMI or generated PINs.

questions.question: Which statement accurately reflects the process for setting a default conferencing option in Notion Calendar?

options.text: You set it at the workspace level, and it applies to all calendars across the account.
options.explanation: Defaults are set per calendar, not globally for the workspace.

options.text: You choose a default tool in Settings → Calendar accounts → Default conferencing for a specific calendar.
options.explanation: Correct – the default is configured per calendar in the Settings sidebar.

options.text: You must add a custom integration first before you can select a default conferencing tool.
options.explanation: Default options can be any supported tool, not only custom ones.

options.text: The default option only works on mobile apps, not on desktop or web.
options.explanation: The article recommends using desktop/web for best experience; defaults work there.

questions.question: Why might Google Meet not appear as a conferencing option in Notion Calendar for a user?

options.text: The user has not connected any Google account to Notion Calendar.
options.explanation: Even with a connected Google account, Meet may be disabled at the admin level.

options.text: Google Workspace admin has disabled Google Meet for the organization.
options.explanation: Correct – if Meet is not enabled in the admin console, it won’t show up.

options.text: Notion Calendar only supports Zoom and Microsoft Teams, not Google Meet.
options.explanation: Google Meet is supported automatically via the connected Google account.

options.text: The user is using the mobile app, which does not display Google Meet options.
options.explanation: The issue is unrelated to the platform; it’s an admin setting.

questions.question: When disconnecting a Zoom account from Notion Calendar, which additional action ensures compliance with Zoom’s data policies?

options.text: Delete all Zoom meetings that were created through Notion Calendar.
options.explanation: Zoom’s deauthorization process handles data; you don’t need to delete meetings manually.

options.text: Remove Notion Calendar from the installed apps list in the Zoom App Marketplace.
options.explanation: Correct – removing the app triggers Zoom’s deauthorization and data‑compliance flow.

options.text: Revoke API tokens from the Zoom developer portal.
options.explanation: While revoking tokens is good practice, the article specifies removing the app from the marketplace.

options.text: Contact Zoom support to request manual data deletion.
options.explanation: Not required; the marketplace removal suffices per the documentation.

questions.question: Which component of the Notion Calendar API schema is optional and can be used to identify the calling application?

options.text: accountEmail
options.explanation: accountEmail is required to identify the Google account.

options.text: iCalUID
options.explanation: iCalUID is required as the event identifier.

options.text: ref
options.explanation: Correct – the ‘ref’ field is optional and can hold the caller’s identifier (e.g., com.example.test).

options.text: startDate
options.explanation: startDate is required to define when the event begins.

questions.question: A user wants to add a custom conferencing tool that isn’t automatically detected by Notion Calendar. Which steps must they follow?

options.text: Go to Settings → Conferencing → Add, give it a name, and paste a stable link.
options.explanation: Correct – custom integrations are added via Settings, named, and any stable link works.

options.text: Create a new calendar account linked to the tool’s API and enable it in Default conferencing.
options.explanation: Custom tools don’t need a separate calendar account; they’re added directly in Settings.

options.text: Contact Notion support to have the tool added to the list of detected services.
options.explanation: Users can add custom tools themselves without contacting support.

options.text: Install a third‑party Notion Calendar plugin from the marketplace.
options.explanation: No marketplace plugin is required for custom links; the built‑in Settings suffice.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about FaceTime integration in Notion Calendar is true?

options.text: FaceTime is supported as a default integration for iOS users.
options.explanation: FaceTime is explicitly not supported.

options.text: You can add FaceTime links as a custom integration.
options.explanation: Even custom integrations cannot use FaceTime because it’s not supported.

options.text: Notion Calendar currently does not support FaceTime as a conferencing option.
options.explanation: Correct – the article notes FaceTime is not supported at this time.

options.text: FaceTime works only when you use Notion Calendar on a Mac.
options.explanation: There is no platform‑specific support for FaceTime.

questions.question: When adding a Notion page to an event’s details, what benefit does this provide?

options.text: It automatically creates a new calendar view inside the Notion page.
options.explanation: It links to the page; it does not create a new calendar view.

options.text: It lets you view important dates from Notion databases directly in Notion Calendar, avoiding duplicate tracking.
options.explanation: Correct – linking Notion pages allows you to see database events without separate tracking.

options.text: It synchronizes the event with all other Notion workspaces you belong to.
options.explanation: The link is local to the workspace; it doesn’t sync across workspaces.

options.text: It enables real‑time video conferencing directly inside Notion Calendar.
options.explanation: Video conferencing is handled by external tools, not by embedding Notion pages.
